sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR FISH AND FISHERY PRODUCTS
sections.summary: Title page and front matter for the Code of Practice for Fish and Fishery Products providing required citation, legal disclaimers, ISBNs, copyright and license information and summarizing the code's role and recent updates (including guidance on minimizing histamine formation).
Author/attribution entry naming Bjørn Røthe Knudtsen as Chairperson of the Codex Committee on Fish and Fishery Products and Regional Director, Norwegian Food Safety Authority; non‑substantive.
Table of contents listing sections and page numbers for the Code of Practice for Fish and Fishery Products; non‑substantive index information.

Introduction stating the importance of fisheries and aquaculture for global food security and that the updated code adds technical guidance to minimize histamine formation across the chain from harvesting to processing to protect health and facilitate trade.

Scope: this Code applies to the growing, harvesting, handling, production, processing, storage, transportation and retail of fish, shellfish and aquatic invertebrates and derived products intended for human consumption.

Scope: This Code applies to the growing, harvesting, handling, production, processing, storage, transportation and retail of fish, shellfish and aquatic invertebrates and products thereof from marine and freshwater sources intended for human consumption.

sections.section: SECTION4   GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE HANDLING OF FRESH FISH, SHELLFISH AND OTHER AQUATIC INVERTEBRATES
sections.summary: Section heading only for 'General considerations for the handling of fresh fish, shellfish and other aquatic invertebrates' (no substantive text provided).

sections.section: 4 General considerations for the handling of fresh fish, shellfish and other aquatic invertebrates
sections.summary: Chapter heading for general considerations on handling fresh fish, shellfish and other aquatic invertebrates; no substantive requirements provided in this entry.
Page number only; no substantive content.

Guidance for vessel-based handling and processing of fresh scallop meat and roe-on scallop meat shucked on board, identifying hazards (microbiological, biotoxins, chemical, physical), defects (dead/damaged scallops, shell fragments, excess water uptake) and step‑by‑step technical controls for landing, washing, shucking, washing shucked scallops, pre‑chilling, packing and chilled storage including water quality/salinity requirements, sanitation, rapid chilling and temperature control (generally 0–4 °C) and limits on voyage/shucking duration to preserve shelf-life.

At scallop landing/deck dump, potential hazards are microbiological, biotoxins and chemical contamination and defects include physical damage or dead scallops; guidance requires disposal of dead/damaged scallops (identified by sensory criteria), reference to Section 7.3, and avoidance of rough handling to minimize stress and death.

For washing live scallops and size grading the hazards are microbiological, chemical and physical contamination and defects include foreign matter and physical damage; washing should use pressurized clean seawater or saltwater prepared from potable water with ~3% food‑grade salt (if not seawater), salinity must be monitored, and scallops should be sorted and graded.

Shucking guidance identifies hazards (physical contamination, marine biotoxins, microbiological) and defects (remaining viscera/roe, damaged or dead scallops, cuts); it requires shucking as soon as possible, disposal of dead scallops identified by sensory evaluation, complete removal of viscera/roe, cleaning/disinfection of hands, tables and knives, worker training to avoid damage, and immediate transfer to washing to limit exposure above 4 °C.

Washing shucked scallops addresses hazards such as shell fragments, marine biotoxins, foreign matter and excess water uptake and directs immediate washing with clean seawater or saltwater made from potable water (3% food‑grade salt if not seawater) with monitored salinity, gentle agitation and separation, restricted contact time if potable freshwater is used, adequate draining, and immediate pre‑chilling/packing with storage at 0–4 °C.

Pre‑chilling guidance to control microbiological hazards and prevent decomposition or excess water uptake directs immediate immersion of scallops in refrigerated seawater or iced seawater (or very limited contact with freshwater ice), periodic replacement of pre‑chill water to control bacterial load and salinity, and maintenance of functional water temperature (≤ 0 °C).
Document title page: 'CODE OF PRACTICE FOR FISH AND FISHERY PRODUCTS' and not a substantive regulatory section.
Document title: 'FISH AND FISHERY PRODUCTS' only; no substantive content in this entry.
Section label indicating Section 20 'PROCESSING OF STURGEON CAVIAR' and a page marker; no substantive text.

Section on processing of sturgeon caviar (applying Standard CXS 291-2010) that outlines hazards (microbial, chemical, physical), defects and control measures, emphasizes HACCP and prerequisite programs, notes microbial controls including good hygiene, potable/clean water, time/temperature control and caviar salt/aw/temperature criteria to inhibit Clostridium botulinum (product salt ≥3 g/100 g; ≥5% salt in water phase; aw <0.97; storage ≤4 °C), and highlights risks from contaminants, packaging hazards and quality defects from handling and additives.
Section header repeating '20 Processing of sturgeon caviar' with no additional substantive content.
Section title and page number for 'Processing of sturgeon caviar' only; no substantive requirements in this entry.

sections.section: 20.3 Belly cutting and ovary removal (Processing Step 3)
sections.summary: Subsection heading '20.3 Belly cutting and ovary removal (Processing Step 3)' only; no substantive text provided here.

sections.section: 20.4 Cutting ovaries into small pieces and sieving (Processing Step 4)
sections.summary: Cutting ovaries into small pieces and sieving identifies microbiological and physical contamination risks and defects (off‑odour, decomposition) and requires brushing the belly with potable/clean water, cleaning/disinfecting utensils with agents that do not affect egg flavour, use of trained personnel, separate knives for belly vs ovary cutting, washable sieves with mesh matching egg size, and cutting ovaries into small pieces to aid sieving while controlling ambient temperature and exposure time.

sections.section: 20.8 Treatment of eggs by shell improving methods (Processing Step 8)
sections.summary: Subsection heading '20.8 Treatment of eggs by shell improving methods (Processing Step 8)' only; no substantive detail in this entry.

sections.section: 20.9 Washing and draining the eggs (Processing Step 9)
sections.summary: Washing and draining eggs notes hazards (chemical, microbiological, residues) and requires that cleaning/disinfection agents not affect egg flavour, shell texturizing agents are not permitted (per CXS 291-2010 Section 4), skilled personnel perform extraction/hand‑stripping, potable/clean and suitably cold water (salt may be added to prevent water uptake) be used, eggs be washed until free of foreign matter, and drained with a sieve to control packaging weight.
Subsection heading '20.10 Ingredients reception (Processing Step 10)' only; no substantive requirements provided in this entry.

Ingredient storage guidance identifies microbiological, chemical and physical contamination and non‑permitted additives as hazards and directs compliance with Section 9.5.1 and CXS 291-2010 Section 4 for additives, inspection and supplier documentation for ingredients, use of food‑grade salt complying with CXS 150-1985, and attention to salt impurities (Mg2+, Ca2+) and fine granule size to ensure rapid dissolution and avoid egg damage.
Section heading only: Packaging reception (Processing Step 12); no substantive requirements provided.

Addresses packaging storage: identifies hazards (microbiological, chemical, physical, packaging material quality, inaccurate labelling, foreign matter) and requires packaging materials to be resistant to caviar components, verified by trained personnel prior to use with supplier documentation, non‑compliant items rejected and corrective actions recorded, labels checked against CXS 1 and CXS 291-2010, and stored in a dry, clean, pest‑free area with periodic monitoring records.
Section heading only: Cleaning of packaging materials (Processing Step 14); no substantive requirements provided.

Provides technical guidance for blending and grading: requires monitoring cleanliness and integrity of packaging, cleaning/disinfection by trained personnel (outside processing area) using potable/clean water and permitted agents with validation/revalidation of effectiveness, use of calibrated equipment to measure eggs/salt/additives, additives to comply with CXS 291-2010 and CXS 192-1995 Section 3, verification of ingredients free from hazardous foreign matter, uniform blending, control of ambient temperature/humidity/duration, and grading by trained personnel; specifies a critical limit to prevent non‑proteolytic Clostridium botulinum of at least 5% water phase salt or water activity < 0.97.
